dialogues: In the Warmup, you saw equal groups lined up into a rectangle. Check out this sticker sheet. The stickers aren't bunched up or scattered around. They're lined up. Every sticker has its own spot. Here's what I notice.
See how they're lined up? They go across, and they go up and down. Everything lines up into a rectangle.
When objects are arranged like this, lined up in a rectangle with every spot filled, that's called an array.
Here's what we call the parts you're looking at. Watch.
See this line going across? That's a row. Rows go across, left to right.
Row 1, row 2, row 3. Three rows going across.
Now this direction. See this line going up and down? That's a column. Columns go up and down.
Column 1, column 2, column 3, column 4. Four columns, all up and down.
Think about the difference. Rows go across. Columns go up and down. How many rows going across does this array have?
Right. 3 rows going across. So this array has 3 rows and 4 columns. An array is equal groups organized into rows and columns.
Here's a new array, this time with dots. How many rows does this one have? Rows go across.
That's right. 2 rows. The lines going across.
Now how many columns? Columns go up and down.
Yes. 6 columns. The lines going up and down. So this array has 2 rows and 6 columns.
This array has 3 rows. Each row has 5 dots. We can say: 3 rows of 5.
Here's how that turns into a multiplication expression.
3 rows of 5. The first number tells us how many rows. That's 3.
The second number tells us how many in each row. That's 5.
3 rows of 5. That's just like saying 3 groups of 5. And you know equal groups means multiplication. So 3 rows of 5 becomes 3 times 5.
Now here's a new array.
Build the expression. First number is how many rows. Second is how many in each row.
Right. 4 times 2. That's 4 rows of 2. The words match the expression.
One more. Check out this array and build the expression for the rows.
Right. 2 times 5. That's 2 rows of 5. You've got it.
You've been describing arrays by rows, those lines going across. But here's the thing about arrays. There's another way to describe them.
You described this array by rows: 2 rows of 5. That gave us 2 times 5.
Here's something interesting -- watch what happens if I look at the columns instead.
I see 5 columns. Each one has 2 dots. So I can also say this is 5 columns of 2.
5 columns of 2 becomes 5 times 2. First number tells me how many columns. Second number tells me how many in each column.
Same array. Different way to describe it, which gives us a different expression. 2 rows of 5 was 2 times 5. 5 columns of 2 is 5 times 2. Both work for this array.
Here's what matters: how you describe the array is what you write. What you say becomes your expression. Let's try one.
This array has 4 rows of 3. But what if we describe it by columns? Count the columns going up and down. Then count how many dots are in each column.
Right. 3 columns of 4. Now write the expression that matches that description.
Yes. 3 columns of 4 becomes 3 times 4. Your description matches your expression.
Look at this array. Let's describe it by rows first. How many rows do you see, and how many in each row? Build the expression.
That's it. 4 times 5 — 4 rows with 5 in each row.
Same array. Now let's flip it — describe it by columns. How many columns, and how many in each column? Build that expression.
Right. 5 times 4 — 5 columns with 4 in each. Same array, two ways to describe it.

Check out these two pictures. On the left, an ice cube tray. On the right, dots. Same number of rows. Same number of columns. Whether you're looking at ice cubes or dots, it's the same array. 2 rows of 6.
The math works the same way no matter what's filling the spots. Build the row expression.
Right. 2 times 6. Ice cubes or dots, the math doesn't change.
You get to choose. You can describe this array by rows, or you can describe it by columns. Either way works. Pick one, then build the whole equation with the product.
First, pick one. Rows or columns?
Now use words to describe what you see, and build the equation to match.
Nice. You went with rows. 3 rows of 4. That's 3 times 4 equals 12.
Now describe it in words and build the equation.
You chose columns. 4 columns of 3. That's 4 times 3 equals 12.

You can describe the same array two ways -- by rows or by columns. Each way gives you a different expression. Now let's flip it. You're going to BUILD arrays. You tell the computer what to make, then find the answer.
Now you get to build arrays. Let me show you how this works.
I want to make 3 rows of 5. I said rows first, so I'll build the rows first.
First row goes in.
Second row.
Third row. Three rows set up, but no dots yet. I need to add columns to fill them in.
Now I add items to each row. Watch this. Each plus puts one dot in every row.
One dot in each row. That's 3 dots total.
Two dots in each row. Six dots. Keep watching.
Three, four, five in each row. Three rows of 5. Fifteen dots. Done.
Now I write the equation.
Three rows of 5. Three times 5 equals 15. I planned it, built it, got my answer. Your turn now.
Let's build an array with 4 rows of 6. Start by making the rows.
That's 4 rows. Now fill in each row with 6 items.
There it is. 4 rows of 6.
Now let's write the equation that matches what you built.
Right. 4 times 6 equals 24. You built the array and solved for the product.
Now try building by columns first. Make 2 columns of 5.
2 columns. Each time you add, you're putting one dot in every column.
You got it. 2 columns of 5. You built it column by column.
Now write the equation for what you just built.
Right. 2 times 5 equals 10. Building by columns works the same way -- the way you describe it matches the expression you write.
Last challenge. I want an array with 20 dots total. You decide: rows first or columns first, pick your numbers, and find the equation. There's more than one way to get this right.
You built it. Now write the word description for what you made.
Now write the equation. What multiplication does your array show?
Right. 4 times 5 equals 20. You chose 4 rows of 5. That's one way to make 20.
You can describe arrays by rows or columns now. You can write expressions for both. And you can find the product. Let's see how you do with this.
